name: Dr. Eleanor Whitmore
myStory: Born and raised in Arkham, Eleanor always felt the weight of the town's strange history. After completing her doctorate at Miskatonic University she returned as a faculty member, drawn by the university's unparalleled collection of rare anthropological texts — some of which she now suspects were never meant to be read.
personalDescription: A composed woman in her late thirties with sharp grey eyes and ink-stained fingers. She carries herself with academic authority, though those who know her notice she rarely sleeps well anymore.
ideologyBeliefs: Knowledge is the only true shield against the unknown. Superstition is simply pattern-recognition without context — but some patterns, she has learned, are better left unrecognised.
significantPeople: Professor Armitage — her former mentor at Miskatonic, now grown paranoid and reclusive. She suspects he knows far more than he tells her.
meaningfulLocations: The Orne Library's restricted stacks. She has spent more hours there than in her own home, and lately the shadows between the shelves seem to move.
treasuredPossessions: A leather field journal filled with annotated rubbings from standing stones in rural Vermont. Several pages are stuck together with something brown and dry.
traits: Methodical, quietly brave, prone to insomnia. Lights a cigarette whenever she opens a tome she is not sure she should be reading.
injuriesScars: A thin scar along the left forearm from an encounter at the Dunwich ruins she refuses to discuss in detail.
phobiasManias: Developing a compulsion to sketch geometric patterns she cannot name — she notices them everywhere now.
arcaneTomesSpells: Has read a partial translation of the Necronomicon (original Latin). Has not yet learned any spells, and is actively trying not to.
encountersWithTheMythos: The Dunwich incident, 1923. She filed an official report attributing the deaths to a gas leak. She has not filed an honest report since.

fellowInvestigators.notes: A journalist from Boston. Resourceful and reckless in equal measure. Has saved her life once; she has returned the favour twice.
